text: The surgeon continues the dissection from medial to lateral above the sacral promontory, initially bridging the peritoneum. The inferior mesenteric artery is dissected from its attachments, cleared, and secured with three hemolog clips—two on the proximal side and one on the distal side. The artery is then detached. Dissection proceeds towards the DJ flexure, detaching Girota's fascia from the colon. The inferior mesenteric vein is dissected near the DJ flexure and clipped similarly. Caution is advised near the tail of the pancreas, with potential entry into the lesser sac.

A swab is placed in the surgical field, and dissection proceeds from lateral to medial to connect with the previous medial to lateral dissection. The gastrocolic ligament is detached from the colon, allowing access to the lesser sac and facilitating complete mobilization of the splenic flexure.

The surgeon continues dissection into the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane posteriorly, using a swab for retraction to avoid breaching the TME package. Dissection proceeds laterally and anteriorly, taking care to avoid nerve damage and protect the prostate and seminal vesicles. The specimen is detached laterally, with the distal margin at the pelvic floor. Suction is used to clear smoke from the narrow pelvis, ensuring a clear view.

The surgeon prepares the area for transection at the pelvic level, performing a sigmoidoscopy to determine the precise transection site. A 45mm Endo GIA stapler is used twice, with minimal blood loss observed during the procedure.

The surgeon retrieves a large specimen from the pelvis, revealing a clean surgical field with intact nerves and presacral fascia. The staple line is visible, and the seminal vesicles remain undamaged. An end-to-end coloanal anastomosis is performed using a circular stapler. A proximal ileostomy is created post-operatively.
